text:
Although research on the Circular Economy (CE) is considered a key driver for global sustainability and climate change adaptation, the CE literature is strongly dominated by publications from the Global North. Research in African contexts is largely an unexplored research landscape. This study therefore aims to fill this gap by developing a preliminary research agenda for CE research in Africa, mapping the current state of CE research in Africa, the research gaps that need to be addressed and the challenges that researchers in Africa encounter.

Title: Critical analysis of existing strategies for advancing sustainable consumption and production in African countries Using a mixed-methods approach, this study conducted a scoping review of 275 academic and grey literature publications in English and French, in addition to administering an online survey to 38 industry experts involved in various CE projects in Africa. The study revealed a concentration of academic and grey literature publications and research on Circular Economy (CE) in South Africa, as compared to other African countries. This calls for more research at the country level in other African countries. Indeed, a good number of publications treat “Africa” as a single economy rather than the 54 diverse economies it has.

Research in the context of circular economy (CE) in Africa focuses heavily on the “downstream” aspect of CE by focusing on materials and products in circulation, implying the need to recycle and manage waste properly. The “upstream” aspects of designing waste and pollution out of the economy and the restoration of ecosystems and the environment remain not well understood and require exploration. Therefore, the paper identifies some specific areas that need to be investigated within the context of opportunities for businesses, inclusive and effective policy frameworks, as well as issues related to digitalisation. This appears to be the first endeavour to formally and comprehensively outline a research agenda focused on CE in diverse African contexts.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
